title: Appendix S1
text:
This appendix provides full technical details for the seven life course methods compared in the main paper, using BMI and systemic inflammation (hsCRP) in the 1958 National Child Development Study (NCDS) as a running empirical example. For each method, we give a brief description, provide implementation choices, and results, with supporting figures and tables. All analyses were conducted in R; code is embedded throughout and can be revealed using the “Show the code” toggle.
Show the code
library(here)
library(gtsummary)
library(gt)
library(mgcv) 
library(tidygam)
library(lcmm)
library(marginaleffects)
source(here("Code", "Clean_NCDS.R"))
source(here("Code", "IOTF_BMI.R"))
set_gtsummary_theme(theme_gtsummary_compact(set_theme = TRUE))
Show the code
# Load the fitted Hierarchical GAM model for BMI predictions
mod.BMI.fs.bam <- readRDS(here("Data", "Models", "mod.BMI.fs.bam.rds"))

# Create predictions from Hierarchical GAM model
df.bmi_prediction <- expand.grid(
  xage = 7:56,
  self_report = 0,
  ncdsid = unique(df.NCDS.HBMI.complete.analytic$ncdsid)) %>%
  left_join(  
    df.NCDS.HBMI.complete.analytic %>%
    select(ncdsid, sex) %>%
    filter(!is.na(sex)) %>%
    distinct() %>%
    group_by(ncdsid) %>%
    summarise(sex = first(sex), .groups = "drop"),
  by = "ncdsid"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    pred_bmi = predict(mod.BMI.fs.bam, newdata = ., type = "response")
  ) %>%
  mutate(sex = ifelse(sex == "Female", "F", "M")) %>%
  left_join(iotf_cutoffs, by = c("sex", "xage" = "age")) %>% 
  mutate(
    bmi_category = case_when(
      pred_bmi < 25   ~ "Normal",
      xage >= 18 & pred_bmi < 30   ~ "Overweight",
      xage >= 18                   ~ "Obese",
      xage < 18 & pred_bmi < overweight  ~ "Normal",
      xage < 18 & pred_bmi < obese       ~ "Overweight",
      xage < 18                          ~ "Obese",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  )

title: Appendix S1
text:
5.1 Separate Models by Time Period


Show the code
df.timeperiod <- df.bmi_prediction %>%
    group_by(ncdsid) %>%
    summarise(
      # Mean BMI by period
      mean_bmi_early_life = mean(pred_bmi[xage >= 7 & xage <= 25], na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_bmi_early_midlife = mean(pred_bmi[xage > 25 & xage <= 40], na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_bmi_late_midlife = mean(pred_bmi[xage > 40 & xage <= 60], na.rm = TRUE),

      # Years in each BMI category - Early life (7-25)
      yrs_normal_earlylife = sum(bmi_category == "Normal" & xage >= 7 & xage <= 25, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_overweight_earlylife = sum(bmi_category == "Overweight" & xage >= 7 & xage <= 25, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_obese_earlylife = sum(bmi_category == "Obese" & xage >= 7 & xage <= 25, na.rm = TRUE),

      # Years in each BMI category - Early midlife (25-40)
      yrs_normal_early_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Normal" & xage > 25 & xage <= 40, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_overweight_early_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Overweight" & xage > 25 & xage <= 40, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_obese_early_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Obese" & xage > 25 & xage <= 40, na.rm = TRUE),

      # Years in each BMI category - Late midlife (41-60)
      yrs_normal_late_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Normal" & xage > 40 & xage <= 60, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_overweight_late_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Overweight" & xage > 40 & xage <= 60, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_obese_late_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Obese" & xage > 40 & xage <= 60, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) %>%
    right_join(df.model, by = "ncdsid")

# Define the 6 BMI predictors to loop through
bmi_predictors <- c(
  "mean_bmi_early_life",
  "mean_bmi_early_midlife",
  "mean_bmi_late_midlife",
  "yrs_normal_earlylife",   
  "yrs_overweight_earlylife",
  "yrs_obese_earlylife",
  "yrs_normal_early_midlife",
  "yrs_overweight_early_midlife",
  "yrs_obese_early_midlife",          
  "yrs_normal_late_midlife",
  "yrs_overweight_late_midlife",
  "yrs_obese_late_midlife"
)

# Run separate time period models and store each model in a list
models_list.timeperiod <- map(bmi_predictors, function(bmi_var) {
    all_covs <- c(bmi_var, covs)
    formula <- reformulate(all_covs, response = "loghsCRP")

    df.timeperiod %>%
      select(all_of(c("loghsCRP", all_covs))) %>%
      rename(exposure = all_of(bmi_var)) %>%  # Rename to generic "exposure"
      lm(reformulate(c("exposure", covs), response = "loghsCRP"), data = .)
  })

saveRDS(models_list.timeperiod, file = here("Data", "Models", "models_list.timeperiod.rds"))

cov_labels <- list(

title: Appendix S1
text:
5.2 Conditional Models of Time Periods



Table S 9: Conditional adjusted regressions of log(hsCRP) with mean BMI from multiple life periods entered jointly, using a stepwise inclusion strategy to assess conditional period-specific effects.



Show the code
# Define the 3 staggered BMI predictor combinations
bmi_predictor_sets <- list(
    "Early Life" = "mean_bmi_early_life",
    "Early + Early Midlife" = c("mean_bmi_early_life", "mean_bmi_early_midlife"),
    "All Three Periods" = c("mean_bmi_early_life", "mean_bmi_early_midlife", "mean_bmi_late_midlife")
  )

# Run conditional time period models and store each table in a list
models_list.timeperiod.staggered.conditional <- map(bmi_predictor_sets, function(bmi_vars) {
    all_covs <- c(bmi_vars, covs)
    formula <- reformulate(all_covs, response = "loghsCRP")

    df.timeperiod %>%
      select(all_of(c("loghsCRP", all_covs))) %>%
      lm(formula, data = .)
  })

saveRDS(models_list.timeperiod.staggered.conditional, file = here("Data", "Models", "models_list.timeperiod.conditional.rds"))






Show the code
cov_labels <- list(
  mean_bmi_early_life ~ "Mean BMI (7-25)",
  mean_bmi_early_midlife ~ "Mean BMI (26-40)",
  mean_bmi_late_midlife ~ "Mean BMI (41-56)",
  sex ~ "Sex",
  sweep10_age ~ "Age at hsCRP measurement (years)",
  educ_qual ~ "Educational qualification",
  father_bmi ~ "Father's BMI",
  mother_bmi ~ "Mother's BMI",
  n646 ~ "Birthweight",
  n607 ~ "Persons per room",
  n2383 ~ "Father's occupation"
)

tbl_merge(
    tbls = map2(models_list.timeperiod.staggered.conditional, bmi_predictor_sets, function(mod, bmi_vars) {
      # Filter labels to only include variables in this model
      model_vars <- c(bmi_vars, covs)
      model_labels <- cov_labels[sapply(cov_labels, function(x) as.character(x[[2]]) %in% model_vars)]

      mod %>%
        tbl_regression(
          intercept = FALSE,
          label = model_labels
        ) %>%
        add_significance_stars(hide_ci = FALSE) %>%
        bold_p() %>%
        add_glance_table() %>%
        bold_labels()
    }),
    tab_spanner = names(bmi_predictor_sets) %>% paste0("**", ., "**")
  ) %>%
    modify_table_body(
      ~ .x %>%
        mutate(
          is_bmi = variable %in% c("mean_bmi_early_life", "mean_bmi_early_midlife", "mean_bmi_late_midlife"),
          is_glance = row_type == "glance_statistic"
        ) %>%
        arrange(desc(is_bmi), is_glance) %>%
        select(-is_bmi, -is_glance)
    )

text:
7.1 Determining Number of Clusters


Show the code
library(WeightedCluster)

# Create a named list of clustering results
cluster_list <- list(
  "2" = cl1.2,
  "3" = cl1.3,
  "4" = cl1.4,
  "5" = cl1.5,
  "6" = cl1.6
)

# Apply wcClusterQuality and extract key metrics
cluster_metrics <- map_dfr(names(cluster_list), function(k) {
  res <- wcClusterQuality(dist.om1, cluster_list[[k]])
  tibble(
    k = as.integer(k),
    ASW = res$stats["ASW"],
    CH = res$stats["CH"],
    R2 = res$stats["R2"],
    PBC = res$stats["PBC"],
    HG = res$stats["HG"]
  )
})

# Reshape for plotting
long_metrics <- cluster_metrics %>%
  pivot_longer(-k, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value")

# Plot
ggplot(long_metrics, aes(x = k, y = value)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ metric, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Number of clusters", y = "Value", title = "Cluster Quality Metrics") +
  theme_minimal()





Figure S 9: Cluster quality metrics (ASW, Calinski-Harabasz, Hubert’s Gamma, Point Biserial Correlation, and R-squared) across 2- to 6-cluster solutions to guide selection of the optimal number of BMI trajectory groups.









ASW (Average Silhouette Width): Measures how similar each observation is to its own cluster compared to other clusters; values closer to 1 indicate better clustering.
CH (Calinski-Harabasz Index / Pseudo F Statistic): Ratio of between-cluster variance to within-cluster variance based on the distance matrix.; higher values suggest more distinct clusters.
HG (Hubert’s Gamma): Measures the correlation between the distances in the data and a binary matrix indicating whether points are in the same cluster (0) or not (1); values closer to 1 reflect stronger clustering structure.
PBC (Point Biserial Correlation): Measures correlation between the pairwise distances and a binary cluster membership indicator (same cluster vs. different cluster); higher is better.
R2 (Explained Discrepancy): Indicates the proportion of total variation explained by the clustering; values near 1 are ideal.

title: Appendix S1
section: 8.3 Predicting Log hsCRP with 3-Class Logarithmic LCTM Using Manual ML Method
text:
8.3 Predicting Log hsCRP with 3-Class Logarithmic LCTM Using Manual ML Method


Show the code
# Implementing the ML-3 Step Method Manually to Account for Classification Uncertainty

# Step 1. Join with posterior class probabilities from hlme
distal_var <- "loghsCRP"

df_person <- df.lgm %>%
  select(ncdsid, all_of(distal_var), all_of(covs)) %>%
  distinct()

pp <- gmmd.bmi.cont.4.log$pprob %>%
  select(ncdsid, class, starts_with("prob"))

# how many classes
Tcl <- length(grep("^prob", names(pp)))
prob_cols <- paste0("prob", 1:Tcl)

analysis_df <- pp %>%
  inner_join(df_person, by = "ncdsid") %>%
  drop_na(!!sym(distal_var))

# posterior matrix tau_it = P(X=t | Y_i)
tau <- as.matrix(analysis_df[, prob_cols])  # N x T
Wobs <- analysis_df$class                   # observed assigned class W_i
N    <- nrow(analysis_df)
Tcl  <- ncol(tau)

# Step 2. Classification error matrix P(W = s | X = t)

# N x T indicator matrix: I(W_i = s)
W_mat <- sapply(1:Tcl, function(s) as.numeric(Wobs == s))  # N x T

R <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = Tcl, ncol = Tcl,
            dimnames = list(paste0("X", 1:Tcl),
                            paste0("W", 1:Tcl)))

for (t in 1:Tcl) {
  denom_t <- sum(tau[, t])
  for (s in 1:Tcl) {
    numer_ts <- sum(tau[, t] * W_mat[, s])
    R[t, s] <- numer_ts / denom_t
  }
}

# R
# rowSums(R)  # should be ~ 1 for each t

pi_hat <- colMeans(tau)   # P(X=t)

# Step 3. Covariate adjustment by residualizing the distal outcome

form_cov <- reformulate(covs, response = distal_var)

lm_cov <- lm(form_cov, data = analysis_df)

analysis_df$Ztilde <- resid(lm_cov)   # covariate adjusted residuals

# Vectors for ML step
Ztilde <- analysis_df$Ztilde
Wobs   <- analysis_df$class

# Step 4. Define the ML 3-step likelihood

logLik_ml3 <- function(par, Ztilde, Wobs, pi_hat, R) {
  T <- length(pi_hat)
  mu <- par[1:T]
  log_sigma <- par[T + 1]
  sigma <- exp(log_sigma)
  
  N <- length(Ztilde)
  ll <- 0
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    s <- Wobs[i]  # observed assigned class (1..T)
    # density of Ztilde_i under each class t (this is the normal distribution assumption)
    dens_vec <- dnorm(Ztilde[i], mean = mu, sd = sigma, log = FALSE)
    # contribution for each class t
    comp <- pi_hat * dens_vec * R[, s]
    p_i <- sum(comp)
    if (!is.finite(p_i) || p_i <= .Machine$double.eps) {
      p_i <- .Machine$double.eps
    }
    ll <- ll + log(p_i)
  }
  
  return(-ll)   # negative log likelihood
}

# Step 5. Optimize the ML 3-step likelihood

mu0 <- tapply(analysis_df$Ztilde, analysis_df$class, mean)
log_sigma0 <- log(sd(analysis_df$Ztilde))

par0 <- c(mu0, log_sigma0)

fit_ml3 <- optim(
  par    = par0,
  fn     = logLik_ml3,
  Ztilde = Ztilde,
  Wobs   = Wobs,
  pi_hat = pi_hat,
  R      = R,
  method = "BFGS",
  hessian = TRUE
)

# fit_ml3$convergence  # should be 0 if successful

par_hat <- fit_ml3$par
Tcl <- length(pi_hat)

mu_hat      <- par_hat[1:Tcl]          # class specific adjusted means
log_sigma_h <- par_hat[Tcl + 1]
sigma_hat   <- exp(log_sigma_h)

vcov_par <- solve(fit_ml3$hessian)
se_par   <- sqrt(diag(vcov_par))

se_mu        <- se_par[1:Tcl]
se_log_sigma <- se_par[Tcl + 1]
se_sigma     <- sigma_hat * se_log_sigma

results_mu <- data.frame(
  class   = 1:Tcl,
  mu_hat  = mu_hat,
  se_mu   = se_mu,
  z_value = mu_hat / se_mu,
  p_value = 2 * pnorm(abs(mu_hat / se_mu), lower.tail = FALSE)
)
# results_mu


# Step 6. Optional: pairwise contrasts vs class 1

get_ml3_contrasts <- function(par_hat, vcov_par, ref = 1) {
  Tcl <- length(par_hat) - 1  # last parameter is log_sigma
  results <- list()
  
  for (t in seq_len(Tcl)) {
    if (t == ref) next
    
    # contrast vector: mu_t - mu_ref
    c_vec <- rep(0, length(par_hat))
    c_vec[t]   <- 1
    c_vec[ref] <- -1
    
    # point estimate
    est <- sum(c_vec * par_hat)
    
    # SE
    se  <- sqrt(t(c_vec) %*% vcov_par %*% c_vec)
    
    # z, p, CI
    z_val <- est / se
    p_val <- 2 * pnorm(abs(z_val), lower.tail = FALSE)
    ci_lo <- est - 1.96 * se
    ci_hi <- est + 1.96 * se
    
    results[[length(results) + 1]] <- data.frame(
      contrast = paste0("class", t, " - class", ref),
      estimate = est,
      std_error = se,
      z_value = z_val,
      p_value = p_val,
      CI_lower = ci_lo,
      CI_upper = ci_hi
    )
  }
  
  bind_rows(results)
}

contrasts_tbl <- get_ml3_contrasts(par_hat, vcov_par, ref = 1) 

# Get labeled class contrasts
contrasts_tbl$contrast <- contrasts_tbl$contrast %>%
    dplyr::recode(
      "class2 - class1" = "Slightly elevated BMI",
      "class3 - class1" = "High BMII",
      "class4 - class1" = "High BMI and Increasing"
    ) 

saveRDS(contrasts_tbl, file = here("Data", "Models", "model.LCA.ML.rds"))




Show the code
contrasts_tbl %>%
    select(-z_value) %>%
    gt() %>%
    tab_header(
      title = "Latent Class Contrasts for log(hsCRP)",
      subtitle = "Reference: Normal BMI Trajectory"
    ) %>%
    cols_label(
      contrast = "Comparison",
      estimate = "β",
      std_error = "SE",
      p_value = "p",
      CI_lower = "95% CI LL",
      CI_upper = "95% CI UL"
    ) %>%
    fmt_number(
      columns = c(estimate, std_error, CI_lower, CI_upper),
      decimals = 3
    ) %>%

title: Appendix S1
section: 10.1 Life Course Hypotheses for BMI Effects on Adult hsCRP
text:
10.1 Life Course Hypotheses for BMI Effects on Adult hsCRP



Table S 15: Life Course Hypotheses for BMI Effects on Adult hsCRP












Hypothesis
Definition
SLCMA Encoding
Algebraic Formulation
Plausible Example for BMI \(\rightarrow\) hsCRP




Critical Period
Exposure at a specific age/stage has the strongest effect on the outcome
Mean BMI within defined age windows (e.g., childhood, early midlife, late midlife)
\(Y = \beta_0 + \beta_j X_j\)
Childhood BMI: BMI during childhood/adolescence (ages 7-18) has the strongest effect on adult hsCRP due to inflammation during formational growth stage


Accumulation
Every additional time point of exposure affects the outcome in a dose-response manner, regardless of timing
Sum of BMI values across all ages/stages
\(Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\sum_j X_j)\)
Cumulative inflammatory burden: Each year spent with high BMI adds to lifetime inflammatory load, with total burden across childhood, adolescence, and adulthood determining adult hsCRP levels


Recency
More proximal exposures (closer in time to outcome) have stronger effects than distal exposures
Age-weighted sum of BMI (more weight to recent measures)
\(Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\sum_j w_j X_j)\) where \(w_j\) = age at time \(j\)
Proximal effects: Late-life BMI has stronger influence on hsCRP than childhood BMI because current adipose tissue mass directly produces inflammatory cytokines


Mobility/Change
Changes in BMI matter more than absolute levels
Change scores between periods (e.g., BMI gain in the last 10 years) or trajectory indicators
\(Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1(X_j - X_{j-1})\)
Weight gain trajectory: Rapid BMI increase in adulthood (upward mobility) triggers greater inflammatory response than stable obesity


Effect Modification
BMI at one life stage modifies the effect of BMI at another stage; interaction between time periods
Product terms between BMI at different ages/stages (e.g., BMI_childhood \(\times\) BMI_adulthood)
\(Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \beta_3(X_1 \times X_2)\)
Priming: High childhood BMI “primes” the inflammatory system, making individuals more sensitive to adult BMI effects (childhood obesity + adult obesity leads to multiplicative hsCRP increase)


Threshold
Only exposure above (or below) a certain threshold affects the outcome; non-linear relationship
Binary indicators for exceeding BMI thresholds (e.g., BMI \(\geq 25\) or \(\geq 30\)) at each time point
\(Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 I(X_j > \tau)\)
Obesity threshold: Only BMI \(\geq 30\) at any point triggers persistent inflammatory changes; being overweight (BMI 25-29.9) has no lasting effect on chronic inflammation


Accumulation + Threshold
Total duration of exposure above a threshold affects the outcome in a dose-response manner
Count of time periods where BMI exceeds a threshold (e.g., number of ages where BMI \(\geq 30\))
\(Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\sum_j I(X_j > \tau))\)
Duration of obesity: The number of life stages spent with BMI \(\geq 30\) determines adult hsCRP; spending 3 periods obese has a greater inflammatory effect than 1 period, regardless of which specific periods






Notation Key:

Y = hsCRP (outcome)
Xⱼ = BMI at time period/age j
β₀ = intercept
βⱼ = regression coefficient for period/age j
∑ⱼ = sum across all time periods
I() = indicator function (1 if true, 0 if false)
wⱼ = weight for period j
D⁺ⱼ,ⱼ₊₁ = upward transition from period j to j+1
D⁻ⱼ,ⱼ₊₁ = downward transition from period j to j+1
τ = threshold value (e.g., BMI = 30)

title: Appendix S1
section: 10.2 Distributions of Variables from each Hypothesis
text:
10.2 Distributions of Variables from each Hypothesis


Show the code
# Define the SCLMA variables based on the chosen hypotheses and merge with the main dataset

df.slcma <- df.bmi_prediction %>%
  group_by(ncdsid) %>%
  select(ncdsid, xage, pred_bmi, bmi_category) %>%
  summarise(
# Threshold + Accumulation
    yrs_normal      = sum(bmi_category == "Normal", na.rm = TRUE),
    yrs_overweight  = sum(bmi_category == "Overweight", na.rm = TRUE),
    yrs_obese       = sum(bmi_category == "Obese", na.rm = TRUE),
# Critical Period
    mean_bmi_early_life = mean(pred_bmi[xage >= 7 & xage <= 25], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_bmi_early_midlife = mean(pred_bmi[xage > 25 & xage <= 40], na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_bmi_late_midlife = mean(pred_bmi[xage > 40 & xage <= 60], na.rm = TRUE),
# Accumulation
    total_bmi = sum(pred_bmi, na.rm = TRUE),
# Recency
    weighted_bmi = sum(pred_bmi * xage, na.rm = TRUE) / sum(xage, na.rm = TRUE),
# Mobility/Change
    bmi_change_late_midlife = mean_bmi_late_midlife - mean_bmi_early_midlife,
# Effect Modification
    bmi_interaction = mean_bmi_early_life * mean_bmi_late_midlife
  ) %>% 
  right_join(df.model, by = "ncdsid")

10.5.2 Selective Inference (Stage 2)


Show the code
# Compute both fixed lasso and relaxed lasso inference for the reduced model at 3rd step
fixed_lasso <- slcma::slcmaInfer(mod.slcma.reduced, 3, method = "selectiveInference")
relaxed_lasso <- slcma::slcmaInfer(mod.slcma.reduced, 3, method = "relax")

order_vec <- c(
  "bmi_change_late_midlife",
  "mean_bmi_early_life",
  "mean_bmi_early_midlife"
)

npred <- length(order_vec)

# Compute Naive OLS estimates with Bonferroni-corrected CIs and p-values
slcma_table <- relaxed_lasso$relax$coefficients %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("Variable") %>%
  filter(Variable %in% order_vec) %>%
  mutate(
    pvalue = 2 * pt(abs(Coef / SE), df = nrow(df.slcma) - npred, lower.tail = FALSE),
    Bonf_pvalue = pmin(pvalue * npred, 1),
    Bonf_CI_lo = Coef + qt(0.025 / npred, df = nrow(df.slcma) - npred) * SE,
    Bonf_CI_up = Coef + qt(1 - 0.025 / npred, df = nrow(df.slcma) - npred) * SE,
    Variable = factor(Variable, levels = order_vec)
  ) %>%
  arrange(Variable)

# Fixed Lasso selective inference results
var_names_fl <- names(fixed_lasso$fli$vars)
fixed_df <- tibble(
  Variable = var_names_fl,
  FL_Estimate = as.numeric(fixed_lasso$fli$coef0),
  FL_pvalue = fixed_lasso$fli$pv,
  FL_CI_lo = fixed_lasso$fli$ci[, 1],
  FL_CI_up = fixed_lasso$fli$ci[, 2]
) %>%
  filter(Variable %in% order_vec) %>%
  mutate(Variable = factor(Variable, levels = order_vec)) %>%
  arrange(Variable)

# Merge
slcma_table <- slcma_table %>%
  left_join(fixed_df, by = "Variable")

saveRDS(slcma_table, file = here("Data", "Models", "model.slcma.reduced.rds"))




Show the code
# GT table

slcma_table %>%
  mutate(
    Variable = case_when(
      Variable == "bmi_change_late_midlife" ~ "BMI change (late midlife)",
      Variable == "mean_bmi_early_life" ~ "Mean BMI (early life)",
      Variable == "mean_bmi_early_midlife" ~ "Mean BMI (early midlife)"
    ),
    `95% CI` = paste0("(", sprintf("%.3f", CI.lo), ", ", sprintf("%.3f", CI.up), ")"),
    `Bonf 95% CI` = paste0("(", sprintf("%.3f", Bonf_CI_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.3f", Bonf_CI_up), ")"),
    `SI 95% CI` = paste0("(", sprintf("%.3f", FL_CI_lo), ", ", sprintf("%.3f", FL_CI_up), ")")
  ) %>%
  select(Variable, Coef, SE, `95% CI`, pvalue,
         `Bonf 95% CI`, Bonf_pvalue,

title: A comparison of methods for estimating the health effects of exposures over the life course: adiposity and systemic inflammation in the 1958 National Child Development Study
text:
This repository accompanies the paper:

A comparison of methods for estimating the health effects of exposures over the life course: adiposity and systemic inflammation in the 1958 National Child Development Study

We compare seven life course modeling approaches for estimating associations between longitudinal BMI trajectories and mid-life systemic inflammation (high-sensitivity C-reactive protein, hsCRP) using data from the 1958 National Child Development Study (NCDS).

text:
Data Access
The 1958 National Child Development Study data are available through the UK Data Service (study numbers SN 2000032 and SN 6137). Access requires registration and approval. The harmonized BMI measures were obtained from the CLOSER Work Package 1 consolidated dataset.
All analyses were conducted in R. The supplementary appendix was authored in Quarto. To reproduce the analyses:

Obtain NCDS data access through the UK Data Service
Run Clean_NCDS.R to prepare the analytic dataset
Render the supplementary appendix (appendix.qmd)
